englishDefinitions: (uncountable) A sense of awe, astonishment and curiosity, inspired by unexpected events, novel experiences and inexplicable circumstances, sometimes accompanied by surprise, shock or reverence.
(countable) Something that causes amazement or awe; a marvel.
An astonishing and seemingly inexplicable turn of events.
A feat that demonstrates incredible finesse or skill, as if performed by magic.
Someone very talented at something, a genius.

(colloquial, sarcastic, following an adjective ending -less that typically has two syllables) A nominalizer that serves as a head noun for the preceding adjective, which together form a mocking or insulting epithet.
(countable, UK, informal) A mental pondering, a thought.
(countable, US) A kind of donut; a cruller.
examples: Socrates: I see, my dear Theaetetus, that Theodorus had a true insight into your nature when he said that you were a philosopher, for wonder is the feeling of a philosopher, and philosophy begins in wonder. He was not a bad genealogist who said that Iris (the messenger of heaven) is the child of Thaumas (wonder).
Wonders of the World seem to come in sevens.
That stay in the country did her wonders.
It's a wonder no-one saw me.
The idea was so ridiculous that it's a wonder anyone went along with it.
Given the limited resources, he's really performed wonders.
So the voiceless wonder speaks at last.
